question.text: Which statement BEST describes the difference between an ETF and an index fund in the Indian context?

answer.text: ETFs are purchased from the AMC at end-of-day NAV while index funds are traded on exchanges
Index funds have lower management costs than ETFs
ETFs are traded on exchanges at real-time prices and require a demat account, while index funds are bought from the AMC at end-of-day NAV
ETFs and index funds are identical products with no meaningful differences

explanation.text: **The Logic:** ETFs trade on stock exchanges at real-time market prices and require a demat account plus brokerage charges. Index funds are purchased directly from the AMC at end-of-day NAV with no need for a demat account. Index funds have marginally higher management costs than ETFs, but ETFs suffer from poor liquidity in India except for the top 2 ETFs by volume. **The Trap:** The first option reverses the characteristics of ETFs and index funds -- a classic trap for candidates who get confused between the two. Index funds actually have marginally higher (not lower) management costs than ETFs. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A extensively covers ETFs and index funds; NISM Series VIII (Equity Derivatives) covers exchange-traded instruments. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** In India, poor ETF liquidity means most retail investors are better served by index funds despite the marginally higher cost. An illiquid ETF can trade at a significant premium or discount to NAV, defeating the purpose of low-cost passive investing.
